summary: Gruber notices that the earphone cables included with the iPhone 3G have a different, more rubbery texture compared to older Apple earphones. After years of tangled white earphone cords, he discovers these new cables are remarkably tangle-resistant. He holds this up as a classic example of Apple's attention to detail. An update reveals the change was primarily environmental — Apple switched from PVC to PTFE for all cables — and the tangle resistance was a pleasant side effect.
key_points: Apple's white earphone cords have been a tangle-prone annoyance for seven years despite subtle design changes
The iPhone 3G earphones use a new rubbery cable material that is significantly tangle-resistant
Gruber frames this as a quintessential example of Apple sweating the details
The actual motivation was environmental: Apple switched from PVC to PTFE, and tangle resistance was a side effect
money_quotes: One thing has remained a constant: the cords get terribly tangled when bunched up in a pocket or pouch.
You can just ball these new ones up into a glob, stuff them in a pocket, and then just shake them straight when you take them out.
I can't help but hold it up as a quintessential example of Apple sweating the details.
The primary reason behind the new earphone cord was environmental.
The increased tangle-resistance was a pleasant side-effect.
key_insight: Sometimes the best user experience improvements arrive as side effects of other good decisions, like Apple's environmental switch from PVC to PTFE cables accidentally solving the perennial tangled-earphone problem.
